quiz.title: Grade-10 Physics: Unit 10 Quiz 7
quiz.subtitle: Magnetic Materials and Properties of Magnets
quiz.subject: Physics

questions.question: One of the following is a ferromagnetic material:
options: Copper
Aluminium
Iron
Oxygen
questions.explanation: Iron is a ferromagnetic material — it is strongly attracted to magnets and can be permanently magnetized. Copper is diamagnetic, while aluminium and oxygen are paramagnetic.

questions.question:
A marine biologist tracking loggerhead sea turtles noticed an unusual behavior pattern during an annual migration across the Atlantic Ocean. Normally, the turtles follow a highly precise, straight migration route by detecting the orientation of Earth's magnetic field lines. However, during a three-day period, a small group of turtles swimming near an underwater volcanic ridge suddenly began traveling in erratic circles and losing their direction. Satellite data showed that Earth's global magnetic field remained completely stable during those three days. Furthermore, water samples confirmed that the ocean temperature, current speed, and chemical composition around the ridge were identical to the rest of the migration route.

Based on the text, what can be most reasonably inferred as the cause of the turtles' temporary disorientation?
options: The underwater volcanic ridge contained highly concentrated magnetic minerals that distorted the local field lines.
A sudden change in the speed of the ocean currents physically pushed the turtles off their straight path.
The turtles temporarily lost their biological ability to sense magnetic fields due to the water temperature.
A global solar storm temporarily disrupted Earth's entire magnetic field network for three days.
questions.explanation: The turtles rely on Earth's magnetic field for navigation. Since the global magnetic field remained stable, and ocean temperature, current speed, and chemical composition were all ruled out as variables, the only remaining factor is the volcanic ridge itself. Volcanic rock often contains high concentrations of magnetic minerals like magnetite, which can create localized magnetic anomalies strong enough to confuse magnetoreceptive animals. Options B and C are directly contradicted by the water sample data. Option D is contradicted by the satellite data confirming the global field was stable.

questions.question:
A scientist cools a metal alloy to a very low temperature and places it in a weak magnetic field. She observes that the material becomes magnetized. When she turns off the external field, the material unexpectedly remains magnetized. During the experiment, instruments recorded a sudden, brief pulse of electric current in the material just before the magnetism locked in place.

What is the most likely explanation for why the material stayed magnetized after the external field was removed?
options: The flow of charge created a magnetic field, which reinforced the magnetization.
The weak external field permanently changed the metal's atomic structure.
At low temperatures, all materials naturally become permanent magnets.
The scientist made an error — magnetism cannot persist without an external field.
questions.explanation: The brief pulse of electric current (flow of charge) generated its own magnetic field. This self-produced field reinforced the magnetization, allowing it to persist even after the external field was removed — consistent with the principle that moving charges produce magnetic fields.

questions.question:
A student conducted an experiment to see how different materials affect the reach of a standard bar magnet. First, he placed a single sheet of regular printer paper between the magnet and a metal paperclip; the paperclip was attracted instantly. Next, he replaced the paper with a thin pane of glass, and then a block of dry wood; in both setups, the magnet easily pulled the paperclip through the barriers. However, when the student substituted a thin sheet of iron between the magnet and the paperclip, the paperclip did not move at all, despite being the exact same distance away. Measurements showed that the magnetic force was entirely absorbed and redirected along the interior of the iron sheet itself.

Based on the results of the experiment, what can be most reasonably inferred about using these materials to construct a protective storage box for electronics that are easily damaged by magnetic forces?
options: A storage box made out of iron would provide better protection for the electronics than a box made out of wood.
A storage box made out of glass would absorb the magnetic forces before they could reach the internal electronics.
A storage box made out of printer paper would block outside magnetic interference more effectively than an iron box.
A storage box made out of any dense material would automatically stop magnetic forces from passing through.
questions.explanation: The experiment showed that paper, glass, and wood allowed the magnetic force to pass through and attract the paperclip. Only iron stopped the magnetic force by absorbing and redirecting it along its own interior. This property — high magnetic permeability — makes iron an effective magnetic shield. Therefore, an iron box would protect electronics from external magnetic fields far better than wood, glass, or paper. Option B is wrong because glass did not block the field in the experiment. Option C is directly contradicted by the result that iron blocked the field while paper did not. Option D is false because wood and glass are dense materials that failed to block the magnetic force.

questions.question:
Atmospheric scientists studying cosmic ray penetration monitored the intensity of Earth's magnetic shield at various research stations worldwide. At the Equator station in Ecuador, sensors recorded the weakest magnetic shielding values, allowing a significant number of solar particles to penetrate the upper atmosphere. Conversely, at the Arctic Circle station in northern Canada, the shielding values were exceptionally high, deflecting almost all incoming particles. When analyzing data from a third mid-latitude station in Madrid, Spain, scientists found that the magnetic shielding strength sat precisely halfway between the values recorded at the Equator and the Arctic Circle.

Based on the data collected by the scientists, what can be most reasonably inferred about the relationship between geography and Earth's magnetic shield?
options: The protective strength of the magnetic shield increases systematically as the distance from the Equator grows.
The magnetic shield is uniformly strong across all latitudes, but solar particle intensity varies with sunlight exposure.
The magnetic shield's strength depends primarily on the local altitude of the research station, not its geographic location.
Solar particles traveling from space physically compress and weaken the magnetic shield at mid-latitudes.
questions.explanation: The data shows a clear gradient: weakest shielding at the Equator, intermediate at mid-latitude Madrid, and strongest at the Arctic Circle. This pattern indicates that magnetic shielding strength increases with latitude — the farther from the Equator, the stronger the protection. Option B is contradicted by the varying shielding values recorded. Option C introduces altitude as a factor, but the passage only compares stations by geographic location, not elevation. Option D is inconsistent with Madrid's intermediate value and the Arctic's high shielding strength.

questions.question:
A team of geologists analyzing ancient volcanic rock layers in Iceland noticed a strange pattern in how iron minerals inside the rocks were oriented. In older layers, the microscopic iron particles pointed toward the current geographic North Pole. However, in a distinct rock layer formed approximately 780,000 years ago, the iron particles completely reversed direction, pointing toward the geographic South Pole. The geologists confirmed that no tectonic shifts had physically rotated the rock layers over time. Furthermore, fossilized plant remains trapped within both layers showed identical structural growth patterns, indicating that the region's local climate and atmospheric conditions remained completely stable during the entire period the rocks were forming.

Based on the text, what can be most reasonably inferred about Earth's magnetic field approximately 780,000 years ago?
options: Earth's magnetic poles flipped orientation during that period.
The iron minerals naturally reverse their magnetic alignment over long timescales without any external cause.
A temporary weakening of Earth's magnetic field allowed solar winds to reorient the iron particles.
The volcanic rock cooled so slowly that iron particles had time to randomly reshuffle their orientations.
questions.explanation: The iron particles in older layers pointed North, but those in the 780,000-year-old layer pointed South. The geologists ruled out tectonic rotation and climate change. The key inference is that Earth's magnetic field itself underwent a complete reversal — not a weakening (option C) or a spontaneous material property (option B). A full 180° reversal indicates a pole flip, not random reshuffling (option D). This matches the real Brunhes–Matuyama geomagnetic reversal.

questions.question:
An engineer was testing a sensitive navigation device that must be completely protected from outside magnetic interference. She placed the device inside a hollow protective sphere made of Mu-metal, a material known for its high magnetic permeability, which normally diverts external magnetic fields around its outer surface. During the first two hours of testing inside a high-temperature chamber set to 800 degrees Celsius, the device functioned perfectly, showing zero magnetic readings. However, toward the end of the third hour, the navigation device suddenly began registering strong, fluctuating magnetic fields from the chamber's heating elements, even though the Mu-metal sphere remained completely intact and uncracked.

Based on the text, what can be most reasonably inferred about the properties of the Mu-metal protective sphere?
options: The extreme heat caused the Mu-metal to lose its ability to divert magnetic fields.
The heating elements gradually increased their power output over three hours until their magnetic field overwhelmed the sphere's protective capacity.
The prolonged exposure to heat caused thermal expansion, creating a temporary gap between the sphere and its sealed base.
The Mu-metal sphere gradually became magnetized by the heating elements' field and began conducting it inward rather than diverting it.
questions.explanation: Mu-metal shields magnetic fields by diverting them around its surface due to its high magnetic permeability. However, when heated beyond its Curie temperature, ferromagnetic materials lose their magnetic properties, including permeability. Since the sphere remained intact and uncracked, and the failure occurred after prolonged exposure to 800°C, the most reasonable inference is that the heat destroyed the material's ability to divert magnetic fields. Option B is contradicted by the sudden onset of interference. Option C conflicts with the sphere being completely intact. Option D describes a process that would defeat the purpose of magnetic shielding — high-permeability materials divert fields, not conduct them inward.

questions.question: The process of losing magnetic property is known as ______________
options: Magnetization
Saturation
Demagnetization
Induction
questions.explanation: Demagnetization is the process by which a magnet loses its magnetic properties. This can occur through heating, hammering, or placing the magnet in a strong alternating magnetic field.

questions.question: Aluminium is classified as ___________ material.
options: Ferromagnetic
Paramagnetic
Diamagnetic
Non-magnetic
questions.explanation: Aluminium is a paramagnetic material. It is weakly attracted to an applied magnetic field and does not retain magnetism when the field is removed. This is due to the presence of unpaired electrons in its atomic structure.

questions.question: _____________ substances are weakly attracted to an applied magnetic field.
options: Diamagnetic
Ferromagnetic
Paramagnetic
Non-magnetic
questions.explanation: Paramagnetic substances (e.g., aluminium, oxygen, platinum) are weakly attracted to an applied magnetic field. This is because they have unpaired electrons that align with the external field.

questions.question: _____________ substances are weakly repelled by an applied magnetic field.
options: Ferromagnetic
Paramagnetic
Diamagnetic
Ferrimagnetic
questions.explanation: Diamagnetic substances (e.g., copper, water, bismuth) are weakly repelled by an applied magnetic field. This occurs due to induced changes in the orbital motion of electrons.

questions.question:
A research team placed a colony of specialized bacteria into a petri dish containing a nutrient gel mixed with a high concentration of microscopic iron particles. In the first phase of the study, the dish was kept in a room completely free of magnetic forces; the bacteria swam uniformly in random directions, distributing themselves evenly across the gel. In the second phase, the researchers placed a powerful permanent magnet directly to the right of the dish. Within one hour, over ninety percent of the bacterial colony had migrated to the far left side of the dish, moving directly away from the magnet. Laboratory analysis of the bacteria showed that their internal cellular structures contained unique chains of synthesized magnetic crystals.

Based on the behavior of the bacteria in the experiment, what can be most reasonably inferred about their biological relationship to magnetic fields?
options: The bacteria possess a natural navigation mechanism that actively steers them away from strong magnetic sources.
The microscopic iron particles in the gel became magnetized and physically pushed the bacteria to the left side.
The bacteria require the presence of an active external magnetic field to successfully synthesize internal crystals.
The permanent magnet destroyed the nutrients on the right side of the dish, forcing the colony to relocate to survive.
questions.explanation: Without a magnet, the bacteria moved randomly. With a magnet on the right, they migrated to the far left — directly away from the magnetic source. Since they contain internal magnetic crystals, the most reasonable inference is that these crystals interact with external magnetic fields to produce a repulsive response, giving the bacteria a mechanism to detect and actively steer away from strong magnetic sources. Option B is unlikely because the iron particles were in the gel, not inside the bacteria, and the bacteria moved under their own swimming power. Option C is unsupported — the crystals were already synthesized before the magnet was introduced. Option D is contradicted by the controlled experimental setup with no mention of nutrient damage.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered magnetic materials, types of magnetism, and the properties of magnets!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review the differences between diamagnetic, paramagnetic, and ferromagnetic materials.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on temporary vs permanent magnets and the classification of magnetic materials.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: types of magnetic materials (ferromagnetic, paramagnetic, diamagnetic), temporary vs permanent magnets, and how magnetic field strength changes with distance.
